---
title: "Carolinas Regional Explorer — Methodology Guide"
subtitle: "Methods, definitions, and caveats for every indicator"
date: last-modified
author: "Kailas Venkitasubramanian"
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
toc-location: left
number-sections: true
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    code-copy: true
    embed-resources: true
    mermaid:
      theme: neutral
---

::: callout-note
**Living document.** An accessible reference for the methodological choices behind the
Carolinas Regional Explorer. **Part I** covers global methods shared across indicators; **Part II** is a
per-indicator catalog. Update whenever methods change or indicators are added/modified. Engineering
details are in the *Data Pipeline Technical Documentation*.
:::

# Scope & geography

The Explorer covers the **14-county Charlotte region**: 11 North Carolina counties (Anson, Cabarrus,
Catawba, Cleveland, Gaston, Iredell, Lincoln, Mecklenburg, Rowan, Stanly, Union) and 3 South Carolina
counties (Chester, Lancaster, York). The **publish geography is the 2020 Census tract** (~752 tracts);
indicators are also rolled up to **county** and **region** for context.

Every indicator is designed to update annually or biennially. Each carries its source and
vintage, a 90% margin of error where applicable, and a reliability flag.

# Part I — Global methodology

## ACS estimates and comparability {#sec-acs}

Most indicators come directly from the **American Community Survey (ACS) 5-year estimates**, published
at the tract level. Two properties drive how we handle them:

- **They are survey estimates with sampling error.** We always carry the margin of error (@sec-moe).
- **5-year vintages overlap.** The 2020–2024 and 2019–2023 estimates share four years of sample, so
  adjacent years are not independent. We therefore treat the annual series as a **rolling 5-year**
  estimate and only compute change and statistical significance between non-overlapping periods:

| Comparable period | Endpoint year used |
|---|---|
| 2010–2014 | 2014 |
| 2015–2019 | 2019 |
| 2020–2024 | 2024 |

The ACS indicators carry the full annual series 2014–2024 (each year is a rolling 5-year estimate).
For a selected tract the app reports two non-overlapping comparable changes, the **5-year**
(latest−5 → latest) and **10-year** (latest−10 → latest), each with the 90% significance test
(@sec-moe). These periods are **derived from the latest year**, so they advance automatically: when
2025 is added they become 2020→2025 and 2015→2025. The non-overlapping endpoints are also derived in
the pipeline (`{latest, latest−5, latest−10}`), so adding a year to `region.acs.annual_years` is the
only change needed.

## Margins of error & reliability {#sec-moe}

ACS publishes a **90% margin of error (MOE)** with each estimate. We convert and propagate it
consistently.

- **Standard error:** $SE = MOE / 1.645$.
- **Coefficient of variation:** $CV = SE / |estimate| \times 100\%$.
- **Reliability flag:** `ok` (CV ≤ 15%), `caution` (15% < CV ≤ 30%), `unreliable` (CV > 30%).

**Propagation (Census formulas).** For derived measures:

- **Aggregated counts** (sum of estimates): $MOE_{sum} = \sqrt{\sum_i MOE_i^2}$.
- **Proportion** $p = num/den$ where the numerator is a subset of the denominator:
  $$MOE_p = \frac{1}{den}\sqrt{MOE_{num}^2 - p^2\, MOE_{den}^2}$$
  If the radicand is negative, we fall back to the ratio formula (per Census guidance).
- **Ratio** $R = num/den$ where the numerator is **not** a subset of the denominator:
  $$MOE_R = \frac{1}{den}\sqrt{MOE_{num}^2 + R^2\, MOE_{den}^2}$$

**Statistical significance of a difference** (90%) between two estimates:
$$\frac{|e_1 - e_2|}{\sqrt{SE_1^2 + SE_2^2}} > 1.645$$
Used only between non-overlapping comparable periods (@sec-acs).

::: callout-tip
At the tract level, small-population rates often have high CVs (frequently `caution`/`unreliable`).
This is inherent to ACS tract data — the app surfaces the MOE band and flag so users interpret
accordingly, rather than hiding it.
:::

CDC PLACES publishes a **95% confidence interval** instead of a 90% MOE; we convert the CI half-width
to a 90%-equivalent MOE ($\times\,1.645/1.96$) so reliability is comparable with ACS.

## Geographic harmonization (2010 → 2020 tracts) {#sec-harmonize}

ACS 5-year estimates through **2019** use **2010 census tracts**; **2020 onward** use **2020 tracts**.
In this region 611 (2010) tracts became 752 (2020) tracts; 126 of the 2010 tracts changed. To make
the time series comparable on a single 2020 geography we build a **population-weighted crosswalk**: each
2010 tract's value is allocated to 2020 tracts in proportion to the **2020 Census block population**
that falls in each piece. Concretely, using the Census 2010↔2020 **block** comparability file, each 2020
block's population is apportioned across the 2010 tracts it overlaps in proportion to the overlap land
area (a dasymetric refinement — a block that straddles two old tracts contributes to both, rather than
being assigned whole to the larger overlap); those apportioned populations are then summed to
$$afact(t_{10}\!\to\! t_{20}) = \frac{\text{2020 block population apportioned to } (t_{10}\cap t_{20})}{\text{2020 block population apportioned to } t_{10}}$$

Allocation happens on the **raw variables before indicator calc**:

- **Counts:** $count_{20} = \sum_{t_{10}} count_{10}\cdot afact$.
- **Margins of error:** $MOE_{20} = \sqrt{\sum (afact\cdot MOE_{10})^2}$ (independence approximation).
- **Medians / averages / indices:** population-weighted average of the contributing 2010-tract values.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  T10[2010 tract A] -->|afact 0.6 by population| T20X[2020 tract X]
  T10 -->|afact 0.4| T20Y[2020 tract Y]
  T10B[2010 tract B] -->|afact 1.0| T20X
```

**Why population (not area):** area weighting assumes people are spread uniformly within a tract, which
badly misallocates tracts that split into pieces of widely different density. For example, a small-but-
dense 2020 tract that holds ~21% of an old tract's people gets only ~0.4% under area weighting but its
true ~21% under population weighting. Population weighting is used by default; an area-weighted crosswalk
remains as a fallback. This harmonization raised comparable-period coverage from ~480 to ~749 of 752
tracts.

**Known limitation — medians.** Medians (income, age) are harmonized as a population-weighted **average of
the contributing tracts' medians**. This is a pragmatic approximation, not the true pooled median: a mean
of medians is biased whenever the within-tract distributions differ in shape, and the bias has no
guaranteed sign. It matters only for the ~126 changed 2010 tracts in pre-2020 years; the unbiased fix is
to reconstruct medians from the afact-weighted **binned** ACS distributions (e.g. B19001 income brackets,
B01001 age brackets) by interpolation, a planned refinement. Readers should treat pre/post-2020 *median*
trends across changed tracts with corresponding caution.

## CDC PLACES time series {#sec-places}

CDC PLACES uses **multilevel regression and poststratification (MRP)** on Behavioral Risk Factor
Surveillance System (BRFSS) data to produce model-based tract-level health prevalence estimates.
Each annual release is a **fresh model fit** — not a revision of prior estimates. See @sec-moe for
the CI-to-MOE conversion applied to all PLACES indicators.

### Multi-release stitching

CRXP draws on **six PLACES releases** (2020–2025). Rather than using only the most recent snapshot,
the pipeline stitches all six into a per-indicator time series using a **newest-release-wins** rule:
where multiple releases report the same data year, the most recent release's estimate is retained.

Per-measure cadence varies across the resulting series:

- **Most established measures** (obesity, diabetes, smoking, mental distress, etc.) span data years
  **2018–2023**.
- **Some biennial measures** appear in odd data years only, e.g. high blood pressure: 2017, 2019,
  2021, 2023.
- **Health-Related Social Needs (HRSN) measures** (food insecurity, housing insecurity,
  transportation barriers, social-emotional support) cover **2022–2023** only.
- **Loneliness** has **2023** only.

### Tract-vintage harmonization (provenance-driven)

PLACES shifted its census-tract geography between releases: the **2020–2023 releases** use **2010
census tracts**; the **2024–2025 releases** use **2020 census tracts**. To unify the series on the
same 2020-tract geography the app uses, the pipeline applies a **provenance-driven** approach: every
fetched row is tagged with its source release's tract vintage, and only 2010-vintage rows are
harmonized; 2020-native rows pass through untouched. This logic is correct regardless of which
data year a given measure happens to report in each release.

Because PLACES publishes **rates, not raw counts**, allocation uses a **population-weighted
pseudo-count**:

1. **Reconstruct cases:** $cases_{10} = (prevalence_{\%} / 100) \times pop_{10}$, where $pop$ is the
   total 2010-tract population (the adult-only denominator `totalpop18plus` is absent from pre-2024
   PLACES releases, so total population is the harmonization weight; the pseudo-count is used
   only as a weight, never published as a denominator).
2. **Allocate via crosswalk:** cases and population are distributed from each 2010 tract to its
   overlapping 2020 tracts using the committed population-weighted `afact` weights (the same
   crosswalk used for ACS — see @sec-harmonize).
3. **Recompute the rate:** $prevalence_{\%,20} = 100 \times \Sigma\,cases_{20} / \Sigma\,pop_{20}$.
4. **Combine MOEs in quadrature:** $MOE_{20} = \sqrt{\sum (afact \cdot MOE_{10})^2}$.

County boundaries are stable across tract vintages, so county data is **not harmonized**; county
releases are stitched newest-release-wins directly. Because the 2020 county release uses a legacy
name-keyed schema with no FIPS codes, the county/region series begins in 2019, one year later than the most-established tract measures begin.

### Temporal comparability: PLACES estimates are *not* for trend analysis {#sec-places-trend}

The multi-release series above is assembled so users can read each year's **level** in context — it is
**not** a basis for measuring change over time, and the platform deliberately shows no change figure for
these indicators. CDC is explicit on this point:

> "The current modeling procedure does not support using the estimates to track changes at the local
> level over time." — CDC PLACES, *Using the Data* FAQ
> (<https://www.cdc.gov/places/faqs/using-data/index.html>)

Two structural features of the model make year-to-year (or release-to-release) comparison invalid:

1. **Sub-county geographies (tract, place, ZCTA) hold the population distribution fixed.** Estimates are
   poststratified to a fixed census population structure rather than re-based annually, so a single
   year's estimate is not a true point-in-time snapshot that can be differenced against another year.
2. **Time is not a model variable.** Even at the county level, where annual population estimates *are*
   used, time is not included in the model, so the series cannot support trend assessment at *any*
   geography.

Compounding this, apparent movement is frequently an artifact rather than real change: the BRFSS
questionnaire and case definitions change between cycles (CDC specifically flags a rise in
colorectal-cancer-screening prevalence caused by a survey-question change), each release re-fits the
model with different BRFSS input years and ACS denominators, and the **2020 BRFSS** is distorted by
pandemic-era data-collection effects. There is therefore **no pair of PLACES releases or data years that
can be validly compared for change** — not across releases (different models/inputs), not within a
release (each measure carries a single year), and not at the county level (time is unmodelled).

**How the platform honours this.** PLACES indicators are flagged in the data contract with
`crossReleaseTrend = true`. Every downstream view that would otherwise express change is suppressed for
them, while the levels and their uncertainty bands are still shown:

| Surface | ACS / measured indicators | CDC PLACES (`crossReleaseTrend`) |
|---|---|---|
| Trend-chart band note | "90% MOE; ACS 5-year estimates are rolling — compare only non-overlapping periods" | "90% model uncertainty interval; read each year's level, not the change between years" |
| Comparable-period change markers (5-/10-year, significance-tested) | shown | **hidden** |
| Custom-area report *Change* column | shown, over each indicator's own period | **rendered as "—"** |
| County-profile card delta ("since YYYY") | shown | **hidden** (the level sparkline is retained) |

This is a direct application of the platform's core principle — *never let a downstream view assert more
certainty than the source supports* (@sec-moe). PLACES model-based estimates are genuinely valuable for
showing **where** health burden concentrates; they are not a longitudinal instrument, and the app
is built to keep users from reading them as one.

## Small-area estimation (current status) {#sec-sae}

We use a **tiered** approach:

- **Direct tract estimates (current):** ACS published tract tables, the great majority of indicators.
- **Model-based small-area estimates (current):** **CDC PLACES** health measures use multilevel
  regression and poststratification (MRP) on BRFSS data, published per tract with confidence intervals.

## Spatial statistics {#sec-spatial}

- **Z-scores:** per indicator/year, standardized to mean 0, sd 1 across tracts.
- **Class breaks:** **quantile** breaks computed **once and held consistent across years** so map color
  means the same value in every year.
- **Spatial weights:** k-nearest-neighbor (k = 8), row-standardized, from each tract's
  **representative point** — a point guaranteed to lie inside the polygon (unlike a bounding-box
  midpoint, which for L-shaped or coastal tracts can fall outside and distort the neighbor graph).
- **Local Moran's I / LISA:** local spatial autocorrelation classified into **High-High, Low-Low,
  High-Low, Low-High,** or **not significant**. Significance is a **conditional-permutation** pseudo
  p-value: tract *i*'s value is held fixed while its hypothetical neighbours are drawn **without
  replacement** from the *other* tracts' z-scores (999 permutations). Because ~752 tracts are tested at
  once, the pseudo p-values are then **FDR-controlled (Benjamini–Hochberg, α = 0.05)** before a tract is
  called significant — without this correction, dozens of tracts would light up by chance alone. The app
  consumes these precomputed quadrants; it does not recompute LISA client-side.

## Raster-derived indicators — zonal statistics {#sec-raster}

Environment indicators come from the **USGS National Land Cover Database (NLCD)**, a 30-metre
satellite land-cover product, accessed auth-free as region-clipped GeoTIFFs from the MRLC Web Coverage
Service (EPSG:5070). Tract values are **area-weighted zonal statistics** computed with `exactextract`,
which uses the **exact fraction** of each pixel falling inside a tract (not just pixel centroids):

- **Class share** (forest, farmland, wetlands, developed): the coverage-weighted fraction of a tract's
  non-nodata area whose land-cover class is in the indicator's class set, e.g. forest = NLCD classes
  41 (deciduous) + 42 (evergreen) + 43 (mixed).
- **Continuous mean** (impervious surface): the coverage-weighted mean of the percent-impervious raster.

NLCD is a **wall-to-wall classification**, so these indicators carry **no sampling margin of error**
(they have classification accuracy instead); the app shows no error band or reliability flag for them.
The **Annual NLCD** product supplies land cover for most years from 2001 onward, so these indicators
carry a multi-year series (the year slider shows the available years) rather than only decadal snapshots.

**Light pollution** uses the **EOG VIIRS Nighttime Lights (VNL V2)** annual composites (median-masked):
the area-weighted **mean radiance** (nW/cm²/sr) per tract, for 2014 / 2019 / 2024. EOG downloads are
account-gated (the global GeoTIFFs are fetched once, manually or via the EOG OAuth API, then clipped
to the region and cached); like NLCD it is a measurement, not a survey, so it carries no MOE.
**Tree canopy** uses the **NLCD Tree Canopy Cover (TCC, USFS)** continuous percent-canopy product
(per-year GeoTIFFs read directly from the supplied zip-of-zips via GDAL `/vsizip/`, clipped once and
cached): area-weighted **mean canopy %** per tract, annual, 2015–2023. Canopy % is broader than the
forest land-cover class (it includes trees in developed/suburban areas), so its values run higher.

## Bivariate analysis {#sec-bivariate}

Two indicators are each split into **terciles** (rank thirds of the raw values; equivalent to terciles
of their standardized values) and combined on a **3×3 grid**, coloring each tract by its joint class.
The legend is interactive: hovering a cell highlights the matching tracts. Useful for seeing where two
conditions co-occur (e.g. high poverty × low insurance).

Alongside the map, a **correlation scatter** plots the two indicators' **standardized (z-score)** values
(one dot per tract, colored by its 3×3 class), with **marginal distributions**, a least-squares
**regression line**, and **both the Pearson and Spearman (rank) correlations**. Spearman is shown
alongside Pearson because most indicators are skewed and the choropleth itself colors by rank. Both are
presented as **descriptive only**: tracts are spatially autocorrelated, so the effective sample size is
far below the tract count and a naïve significance test would be badly anti-conservative; the scatter
therefore reports no p-value. Hovering a dot highlights the tract on the map (and vice-versa) and shows
its neighborhood name.

## County and region estimates {#sec-agg}

County and region values are **not** computed by averaging tracts — that is biased for rates (it
over-weights small tracts) and invalid for medians and indices (a county median cannot be recovered from
tract medians). Instead:

- **County** = the **authoritative published county estimate** from each source. County boundaries are
  stable across 2010/2020, so the engine fetches each county directly from ACS county tables (with the smaller
  county MOE), the CDC PLACES county model (`swc5-untb`), or county zonal statistics for satellite measures
  (land-area-weighted tract values, which equal the county zonal value), and runs the **same** indicator
  calculator used for tracts. This gives the correct value and a proper county MOE for every kind.
- **Region** = pooled by indicator kind: **counts** are summed; **rates/proportions** use the pooled
  numerator ÷ pooled denominator (with the Census proportion/ratio MOE on the summed components, the MOE
  variables combined in quadrature); **medians and indices** use a population-weighted mean of the county
  values (a documented approximation, as an exact regional median needs microdata).

This is implemented in `aggregate.build_county_region`, persisted to a `county_indicators` warehouse table
(geoid = county FIPS, plus a `REGION` row), and consumed by `export_crxp.build_aggregates` →
`aggregates.json` (`countyAvg`/`regionAvg` with `countyMoe`/`regionMoe`). The app labels these lines
**County** and **Region**.

**Note.** The one remaining simple unweighted mean is the *ad-hoc average of a user-selected set of tracts*
in a custom report (`series.js seriesForSet`) — a selection summary, not a published geography.

## Density and land area {#sec-density}

Density indicators divide a count by **2020 tract land area in square miles** (from the Census
relationship files, water excluded). Because both estimate and MOE are divided by the same constant,
**CV and reliability are unchanged**.

## Data sources & sustainability {#sec-sources}

| Source | Used for | Cadence | Sustainability |
|---|---|---|---|
| ACS 5-year (Census API / Census Reporter) | demographics, economy, education, housing, transportation, insurance, disability | annual (rolling) | active |
| CDC PLACES | adult health & wellbeing (16 measures surfaced; 40 staged) | multi-release series (2020–2025 releases; 2017–2023 data years) | active |
| USGS NLCD (via MRLC Web Coverage Service, WCS) | environment (forest, farmland, wetlands, developed, impervious) | ~biennial series (2001–2021) | active |
| EOG VIIRS Nighttime Lights (VNL V2) | environment (light pollution) | annual (2012–) | active (account-gated download) |

Some historically useful tract sources are **static / discontinued** and, if added later, must be
labeled with their last vintage: Eviction Lab (≤2018), USDA Food Access (2019), HUD LAI (2019),
USALEEP life expectancy (2010–2015). (The ACS *subject* table S1401 jams its public/private split at the
tract level, but the **detailed table B14002** publishes it cleanly, so *Private school enrollment* (id 13)
is a current, fully reproducible indicator with a margin of error — see its row above.)

# Part II — Indicator catalog

Definitions below are the canonical reference. Direction "—" means neither higher nor lower is
inherently better. All ACS indicators are 2020-tract, harmonized (@sec-harmonize), with MOE
(@sec-moe); CDC indicators are model-based (@sec-sae).

*65 indicators across 8 of 10 themes (Safety and Arts & Culture not yet populated).*

### Character

| ID | Indicator | Source | Table/Measure | Kind | Direction | Definition |
|----|-----------|--------|---------------|------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | Older adults (65+) | ACS | B01001 | proportion | — | Share of residents aged 65 and older |
| 2 | Youth (under 18) | ACS | B01001 | proportion | — | Share of residents under age 18 |
| 3 | Veterans | ACS | B21001 | proportion | — | Share of civilian adults who are military veterans |
| 4 | Asian residents | ACS | B03002 | proportion | — | Residents who are Asian (non-Hispanic) |
| 5 | Black residents | ACS | B03002 | proportion | — | Residents who are Black or African American (non-Hispanic) |
| 6 | Hispanic or Latino residents | ACS | B03002 | proportion | — | Residents who are Hispanic or Latino |
| 7 | White residents | ACS | B03002 | proportion | — | Residents who are White (non-Hispanic) |
| 8 | Other races | ACS | B03002 | proportion | — | Residents of other or multiple races (non-Hispanic) |
| 21 | Median age | ACS | B01002 | median | — | Median age of residents, in years |
| 34 | Limited-English-speaking households | ACS | C16002 | proportion | more need | Households where no one 14+ speaks English "very well" |
| 50 | Total population | ACS | B01003 | count | — | Total resident population |
| 51 | Population density | ACS | B01003 ÷ land area | density | — | Residents per square mile of land area (@sec-density) |
| 53 | Households with children | ACS | B11005 | proportion | — | Households with one or more people under 18 |
| 54 | Households with seniors | ACS | B11007 | proportion | — | Households with one or more people 65+ |
| 63 | Foreign-born residents | ACS | B05012 | proportion | — | Residents born outside the United States |

### Economy

| ID | Indicator | Source | Table/Measure | Kind | Direction | Definition |
|----|-----------|--------|---------------|------|-----------|------------|
| 9 | Employment rate | ACS | B23025 | proportion | better | Employed ÷ population age 16+ (employment-population ratio) |
| 10 | Households with internet access | ACS | B28002 | proportion | better | Households with a broadband internet subscription |
| 20 | Median household income | ACS | B19013 | median | better | Median annual household income |
| 30 | Poverty rate | ACS | B17001 | proportion | more need | People with income below the federal poverty level |
| 31 | Unemployment rate | ACS | B23025 | proportion | more need | Unemployed ÷ civilian labor force |
| 33 | Households receiving SNAP | ACS | B22010 | proportion | more need | Households that received SNAP in the past year |
| 57 | Income inequality (Gini) | ACS | B19083 | index | more need | Gini index of income inequality (0 = equal, 1 = unequal) |
| 59 | Management & professional occupations | ACS | C24010 | proportion | — | Employed residents in management, business, science & arts occupations (@sec-whitecollar) |
| 64 | Child poverty (families w/ children) | ACS | B17010 | proportion | more need | Families with related children <18 below poverty ÷ all families with related children (@sec-childpov) |

### Education

| ID | Indicator | Source | Table/Measure | Kind | Direction | Definition |
|----|-----------|--------|---------------|------|-----------|------------|
| 11 | Bachelor's degree or higher | ACS | B15003 | proportion | better | Adults 25+ with a bachelor's degree or higher |
| 12 | High school diploma or higher | ACS | B15003 | proportion | better | Adults 25+ with at least a high school diploma |
| 58 | School enrollment (age 3+) | ACS | B14001 | proportion | — | Population age 3+ enrolled in school |
| 13 | Private school enrollment | ACS | B14002 | proportion | — | Students enrolled in private school ÷ all students enrolled (private + public, all levels) (@sec-legacy) |

### Health

| ID | Indicator | Source | Table/Measure | Kind | Direction | Definition |
|----|-----------|--------|---------------|------|-----------|------------|
| 40 | No health insurance (adults 18–64) | CDC PLACES | ACCESS2 | model-based | more need | Adults 18–64 without health insurance |
| 41 | Obesity (adults) | CDC PLACES | OBESITY | model-based | more need | Adults with obesity (BMI ≥ 30) |
| 42 | Diabetes (adults) | CDC PLACES | DIABETES | model-based | more need | Adults ever told they have diabetes |
| 43 | Frequent mental distress (adults) | CDC PLACES | MHLTH | model-based | more need | Adults with 14+ days of poor mental health in the past month |
| 44 | Depression (adults) | CDC PLACES | DEPRESSION | model-based | more need | Adults ever diagnosed with depression |
| 45 | High blood pressure (adults) | CDC PLACES | BPHIGH | model-based | more need | Adults ever told they have high blood pressure |
| 46 | Current asthma (adults) | CDC PLACES | CASTHMA | model-based | more need | Adults who currently have asthma |
| 47 | Current smoking (adults) | CDC PLACES | CSMOKING | model-based | more need | Adults who currently smoke cigarettes |
| 48 | No leisure-time physical activity | CDC PLACES | LPA | model-based | more need | Adults reporting no leisure-time physical activity |
| 49 | Fair or poor health (adults) | CDC PLACES | GHLTH | model-based | more need | Adults reporting fair or poor general health |
| 65 | Routine checkup (adults) | CDC PLACES | CHECKUP | model-based | better | Adults with a routine checkup in the past year |
| 66 | Food insecurity (adults) | CDC PLACES | FOODINSECU | model-based | more need | Adults experiencing food insecurity in the past year |
| 67 | Housing insecurity (adults) | CDC PLACES | HOUSINSECU | model-based | more need | Adults experiencing housing insecurity in the past year |
| 68 | Transportation barriers (adults) | CDC PLACES | LACKTRPT | model-based | more need | Adults lacking reliable transportation in the past year |
| 69 | Lack of social & emotional support | CDC PLACES | EMOTIONSPT | model-based | more need | Adults who lack needed social and emotional support |
| 77 | Loneliness (adults) | CDC PLACES | LONELINESS | model-based | more need | Adults who report feeling lonely |
| 61 | Health insurance coverage | ACS | B27001 | proportion | better | Residents (all ages) with health insurance (@sec-insurance) |
| 62 | Disability (any) | ACS | B18101 | proportion | — | Civilian noninstitutionalized residents with a disability |

### Engagement

| ID | Indicator | Source | Table/Measure | Kind | Direction | Definition |
|----|-----------|--------|---------------|------|-----------|------------|
| 81 | Residential stability | ACS | B07001 | proportion | — | Residents who lived in the same home one year ago (a proxy for rootedness, not direct civic participation) |

### Housing

| ID | Indicator | Source | Table/Measure | Kind | Direction | Definition |
|----|-----------|--------|---------------|------|-----------|------------|
| 14 | Owner-occupied homes | ACS | B25003 | proportion | better | Occupied homes that are owner-occupied |
| 15 | Occupied homes | ACS | B25002 | proportion | — | Housing units that are occupied |
| 16 | Vacant homes | ACS | B25002 | proportion | more need | Housing units that are vacant |
| 32 | Rent-burdened households | ACS | B25070 | proportion | more need | Renter households paying 30%+ of income on gross rent (of those with a computable rent-to-income ratio) |
| 52 | Average household size | ACS | B25010 | average | — | Average people per occupied household |
| 55 | Owner cost burden | ACS | B25091 | proportion | more need | Owner households paying 30%+ of income on housing costs (of those with a computable cost-to-income ratio) |
| 56 | Homes built since 2010 | ACS | B25034 | proportion | — | Housing units built in 2010 or later (@sec-newhomes) |

### Transportation

| ID | Indicator | Source | Table/Measure | Kind | Direction | Definition |
|----|-----------|--------|---------------|------|-----------|------------|
| 17 | Households without a vehicle | ACS | B08201 | proportion | more need | Households with no vehicle available |
| 18 | Commute over 20 minutes | ACS | B08303 | proportion | more need | Workers with a commute of 20+ minutes |
| 19 | Drove alone to work | ACS | B08301 | proportion | — | Workers who commute by driving alone |
| 60 | Mean travel time to work | ACS | B08013 ÷ B08303 | rate | more need | Average one-way commute time, minutes (@sec-traveltime) |

### Environment

Raster-derived (NLCD), area-weighted zonal statistics (@sec-raster); no sampling MOE. Land-cover/impervious
indicators carry the ~biennial NLCD series (2001, 2004, 2006, 2008, 2011, 2013, 2016, 2019, 2021); tree
canopy is annual (2015–2023) from NLCD Tree Canopy Cover.

| ID | Indicator | Source | Classes/stat | Direction | Definition |
|----|-----------|--------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| 70 | Forest cover | NLCD land cover | classes 41,42,43 | — | Share of land that is forest (deciduous/evergreen/mixed) |
| 71 | Cropland & farmland | NLCD land cover | classes 81,82 | — | Share of land in agriculture (pasture/hay + cultivated crops) |
| 72 | Wetlands | NLCD land cover | classes 90,95 | — | Share of land that is wetland (woody + emergent herbaceous) |
| 73 | Developed land | NLCD land cover | classes 21–24 | more need | Share of land developed (open space → high intensity) |
| 74 | Impervious surface | NLCD impervious | mean % | more need | Average share of surface that is impervious |
| 75 | Light pollution | EOG VIIRS VNL V2 | mean radiance | more need | Mean nighttime-light radiance (nW/cm²/sr); 2014/2019/2024 (three vintages surfaced from an annual source) |
| 76 | Tree canopy | NLCD Tree Canopy Cover | mean % | better | Mean continuous tree-canopy cover; annual 2015–2023 |

## Indicator notes (special cases)

### Management & professional occupations ("white-collar") {#sec-whitecollar}
Defined as the Census **"Management, business, science, and arts"** occupational major group
(C24010, male + female) ÷ employed civilians 16+. This major group is the standard proxy for
**professional/managerial ("white-collar")** work in labor-market and social-stratification research —
it captures the professional-managerial class and is distinct from sales/office, service, and
production/transportation (blue-collar) groups. A broader "white-collar" definition would additionally
include sales-and-office occupations; we report the professional-managerial core for interpretability
and consistency with the literature.

### Health insurance: two complementary measures {#sec-insurance}
- **No health insurance, adults 18–64 (id 40, CDC PLACES):** model-based adult uninsured prevalence.
- **Health insurance coverage, all ages (id 61, ACS B27001):** direct ACS measure across all ages.

They differ in population (adults vs all ages), method (model-based vs direct), and direction
(uninsured vs insured). Use id 61 for an all-ages coverage rate; id 40 for the adult uninsured story.

### Child poverty (id 64) {#sec-childpov}
Numerator: families with related children <18 with income below poverty (married-couple + male- and
female-householder families). Denominator: **all** families with related children <18 (the summed
six-line denominator across below- and at/above-poverty branches of B17010). This is a *family-based*
child-poverty measure (families with children in poverty), complementary to the overall poverty rate
(id 30).

### Homes built since 2010 (id 56) {#sec-newhomes}
From the B25034 "year structure built" distribution, we report the share built **2010 or later** as a
**growth/new-construction** signal, especially informative for this region's fast-growing exurbs.
Alternatives (median year built; share built before 1980 for housing-age/condition) can be added if
needed.

B25034 **redefines its category boundaries by vintage**, so a fixed set of variable indices does not
mean the same thing every year. From the 2015 ACS5 onward the two newest categories together span
exactly "built 2010 or later," but in the **2014 ACS5** those same indices are "Built 2010 or later"
plus "Built 2000 to 2009"; summing them would sweep in two extra decades and inflate 2014. The indicator
therefore uses a **per-vintage numerator override** (`num_vars_by_year` in the config): for 2014 the
numerator is the single "2010 or later" category; for 2015–2024 it is the two newest categories. This
keeps the series measuring the same quantity in every year and is the one indicator where a fixed
variable list would silently change meaning across vintages.

### Mean travel time to work (id 60) {#sec-traveltime}
Aggregate travel time (B08013) ÷ workers who commute (B08303), giving mean one-way minutes. This is a
**rate** (numerator not a subset of the denominator), so the ratio-MOE formula applies. It complements
the categorical "commute over 20 minutes" (id 18).

### Private school (id 13) — recreated as a current indicator {#sec-legacy}
Originally a legacy orphan (committed app values only, no config, no margin of error) because the ACS
*subject* table S1401 jams its public/private split at the tract level. It has been **rebuilt from the
detailed table B14002** (private-school enrollees across all levels ÷ all enrolled students), so it now
carries the full 2014–2024 series, a margin of error and reliability flags, and an authoritative county
estimate like every other indicator.

### Median harmonization caveat {#sec-medians}
For the approximation applied to medians, averages, and indices on pre-2020 split/merged tracts, see
@sec-harmonize ("Known limitation — medians").
